content.text: Multi-Model Routing Policy and Fallback Rules

is the runtime logic that decides which AI model handles each request when your architecture includes more than one model — Claude Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, or models from other vendors. Done well, routing cuts inference cost by 40–70 % while preserving quality where it matters. Done poorly, it creates cascading failures, inconsistent user experiences, and opaque debugging.
This topic covers how to design routing policies, define fallback chains, and operationalize the decision logic in production Claude deployments.
Routing is not optimization later — it is architecture now. The fallback chain you define at design time determines your system's behavior at 3 AM during a model outage.
Enterprises running Claude at scale quickly discover that a single-model, single-vendor approach creates three problems:
Running every request through the highest-capability model wastes 60–80 % of inference budget on tasks that don't need it.
A single model endpoint becomes a single point of failure. Model API outages are not rare — major providers report 1–4 incidents per quarter.
Some tasks need deep reasoning (Opus); others need speed (Haiku). A uniform model choice means you are either over-spending or under-performing.
Multi-model routing solves all three — but requires explicit policy design rather than ad-hoc if/else logic scattered through application code.

attrs.alt: Multi-model routing policy decision flow with primary models and fallback chains

content.text: A complete routing policy has four layers:
Classification — determine the nature of the incoming request
Selection — choose the primary model based on classification
Execution — call the model with circuit-breaker protection
Fallback — handle unavailability or SLA violations gracefully
Classification is the most important and often the least-designed layer. Without good classification, routing decisions are arbitrary. Classification signals include:
Request metadata field or NLP classifier
Data classification tag in request context
User/org subscription level or explicit flag
(a cheap model or rule-based classifier evaluates the request before routing), or
(static overrides take precedence; dynamic classification fills gaps). Dynamic classification adds 50–200ms latency; use it only when static metadata is insufficient.
Once a request is classified, a selection matrix maps classifications to models. Define this matrix explicitly in a configuration file — not in code.

# routing-policy.yaml
routing:
  rules:
    - name: deep-reasoning
      match:
        task_type: [reasoning, complex-analysis, code-review]
        sla_tier: [professional, standard]
      model: claude-opus-4-8
      max_tokens: 8192

    - name: standard-workloads
      match:
        task_type: [summarization, drafting, qa, extraction]
        sla_tier: [standard, professional]
      model: claude-sonnet-5
      max_tokens: 4096

    - name: high-volume-low-latency
      match:
        task_type: [classification, short-generation, intent-detection]
        sla_tier: [realtime]
      model: claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
      max_tokens: 1024

    - name: catch-all
      match: {}
      model: claude-sonnet-5
      max_tokens: 4096

  # Rules evaluated top-to-bottom; first match wins
  match_order: first-match

is mandatory. Without it, an unclassified request has undefined behavior. The catch-all should point to your most versatile model, not your most capable — Sonnet is the right default for most organizations.
Every model call in a routed system needs a circuit breaker. Without one, a degraded model endpoint cascades into application-wide failures as threads pile up waiting for timeouts.
The fallback chain defines what happens when the primary model is unavailable, times out, or returns an error. Design it as a linear chain, not a tree — complexity grows exponentially with branching.
Every fallback response must be tagged with a
). Downstream systems and dashboards must be able to distinguish normal responses from fallback responses — SLA measurement should exclude or flag fallback traffic.
A routing policy is not a one-time configuration — it is a living document that requires governance:
Store routing-policy.yaml in your main repository alongside application code. Every policy change should go through code review.
Deploy routing policy changes with a feature flag. Route 5 % of traffic to the new policy, validate cost and quality metrics, then ramp to 100 %.
Before promoting a new routing rule to production, run it in shadow mode — process real requests but don't use the shadow result. Compare model selection and cost projections against the baseline.
Write unit tests for the classification + selection logic. Each test asserts that a given (task_type, sla_tier, token_budget) tuple maps to the expected model.

# tests/test_routing_policy.py
import pytest
from router import route_request  # your router module

ROUTING_CASES = [
    # (task_type, expected_primary_model)
    ("reasoning",         "claude-opus-4-8"),
    ("complex-analysis",  "claude-opus-4-8"),
    ("summarization",     "claude-sonnet-5"),
    ("classification",    "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"),
    ("unknown-task",      "claude-sonnet-5"),  # catch-all
]

@pytest.mark.parametrize("task_type,expected_model", ROUTING_CASES)
def test_primary_model_selection(task_type, expected_model):
    \"\"\"Verify routing policy selects correct primary model.\"\"\"
    policy = load_routing_policy()
    model, _ = policy.select(task_type)
    assert model == expected_model, (
        f"task_type={task_type!r}: expected {expected_model!r}, got {model!r}"
    )
Multi-model routing only saves money if you can measure it. Instrument every request with:
— the actual model that handled the request
— the classification that drove model selection
— wall-clock time from dispatch to response
Aggregate these metrics to compute weekly cost-per-task-type and quality trends. The canonical dashboard shows:
Investigate primary model health; consider failover pre-emption
Cost per 1K requests by task type
Review classification accuracy; check for task type drift
Check model queue depth; consider pre-warming or capacity increase
Escalate to vendor; check contract SLA credits
Hard-coding model names in application code.
Model identifiers change on upgrade. Store model selection in config files, not source code.
Without explicit timeouts, a slow model response hangs threads indefinitely. Set timeout to 2–3× expected P99 latency.
If your fallback calls the same endpoint or region as the primary, a network partition takes both down. Fallbacks should use a distinct endpoint or region.
A fallback response that reaches the user with no indication it is degraded erodes trust and makes SLA measurement impossible.
Routing decisions in the UI layer.
Routing belongs in a dedicated service or middleware — not scattered across frontend or orchestration layers where it is impossible to audit or change uniformly.

Multi-model routing is the runtime expression of your build vs buy and model selection decisions. A well-designed policy uses a classification layer to match each request to its best-fit model, a circuit breaker to prevent cascading failures, and a linear fallback chain to degrade gracefully when primary models are unavailable. Maintain the routing matrix as versioned configuration, test it with explicit assertions, and instrument every response with the metadata needed to attribute cost, measure SLA compliance, and detect quality drift.
